reasoning: ke- appears whenever a 2nd person participant is present; -i marks 1st-person object, and -ena:n gives 1pl (excl). Absence of -wa: indicates 2sg, so kewa:pamiehmena:n = ‘you (sg) see us (excl)’.
With no 2nd person, ne- is used for 1st person. 3rd-person object uses -a: before -ehm; ‘them’ adds -aki. ‘We (excl)’ adds -ena:n. Hence ‘we see them’ → newa:pama:ehmena:naki.
When a 3rd person acts on a 1st person, the inverse marker -ekw- appears before -ehm. With 3pl subject add -aki. Thus ‘they see me’ → newa:pamekwehmaki.
Forms involving 1st but not 2nd person (e.g., ‘I/We …’ with 3rd) start with ne- (newa:…).
-wa: appears whenever the 2nd person is plural, regardless of subject vs. object (e.g., ‘I see you (pl)’, ‘you (pl) see me’).
The segment -eθ occurs only when ‘you’ is the object (e.g., ‘I/we see you’), with number supplied separately by -wa: for plural.
-i shows up when the object is 1st person (e.g., ‘you see me’; also co-occurs with -ena:n for 1pl excl object).
-ehm is the common transitive ending present in all forms, following the object marker (and preceded by the inverse -ekw- when applicable).
-aki marks 3rd person plural, whether subject (‘they see …’) or object (‘… see them’).

answer: ke- occurs whenever a 2nd person is present; ne- is used when 1st person participates and no 2nd person does. The object is marked immediately before -ehm: -i (1st), -eθ (2nd), -a: (3rd). Number markers follow -ehm: -wa: (2pl) and -ena:n (1pl excl). Third plural is -aki at the very end. When a 3rd person acts on a 1st person, the inverse marker -ekw- appears before -ehm. Applying these pieces yields the translations in P1.
reasoning: Summary of the morphological pattern that consistently derives the given forms.
